name: NYC Local Law 144 (Automated Employment Decision Tools)
description: New York City law requiring an independent bias audit, public disclosure of audit results, and candidate notice for automated employment decision tools (AEDTs) used to substantially assist or replace discretionary decisions in hiring or promotion for New York City-based candidates or employees.
verification_note: Verified via web search 2026-07-09 (Week 2); re-verified 2026-07-10 (Week 3 and Week 4 parallel cleanups) against the official NYC Administrative Code library (codelibrary.amlegal.com, via search-indexed content -- direct fetch returned HTTP 403) and a secondary source quoting the DCWP final rule's carve-out language directly (National Law Review analysis of the final regulations). Citations: section 20-870 = Definitions (including 'bias audit' and 'employment decision'); section 20-871 = the operative prohibition on using an AEDT without a bias audit, public summary, and notice. Local Law 144 was enacted 2021-12-11 and took effect 2023-01-01, with active enforcement beginning 2023-07-05 -- effective_from=2023-01-01 represents when obligations legally began binding, not the later enforcement-start date. CARVE-OUT LANGUAGE RESOLVED 2026-07-10 (Week 4): the DCWP final rule's own carve-out, quoted via secondary source: 'The definition does not include tools that do not automate, support, substantially assist, or replace discretionary decision-making processes and that does not materially impact natural persons, such as a junk email filter, firewall, antivirus software, spreadsheet, or other compilation of data.' The rule does NOT further quantify 'substantially assist' beyond this exclusion-by-example approach -- that is the rule's actual design (a qualitative, example-bounded standard), not a gap in this verification. Scope of 'employment decision' confirmed broader than final hiring alone: covers early-stage screening, promotion, and any decision materially impacting a candidate or employee, not solely an ultimate hire/no-hire determination. As of 2026, a December 2025 NY State Comptroller audit found weak DCWP oversight, prompting stricter 2026 enforcement (an enforcement-posture change, not a substantive-requirement change). NEEDS_OWNER_REVIEW (narrowed, not cleared): the precise statutory citation for the carve-out examples above (which section of the DCWP final rule, as opposed to the Admin Code itself) was not confirmed -- the final rule PDF could not be parsed by this session's tooling (binary/compressed content). Sources: NYC Administrative Code Sec. 20-870/20-871 (amlegal.com, https://codelibrary.amlegal.com/codes/newyorkcity/latest/NYCadmin/0-0-0-135839); National Law Review, 'NYC's Local Law 144 and the Final Regulations' (https://natlawreview.com/article/nyc-s-local-law-144-and-final-regulations-regulation-ai-driven-hiring-tools-united); NYC DCWP, Automated Employment Decision Tools (https://www.nyc.gov/site/dca/about/automated-employment-decision-tools.page); Deloitte, 'NYC Local Law 144-21 and Algorithmic Bias' (https://www.deloitte.com/us/en/services/audit-assurance/articles/nyc-local-law-144-algorithmic-bias.html); NY State Comptroller, 'Enforcement of Local Law 144' (2025-12-02) (https://www.osc.ny.gov/state-agencies/audits/2025/12/02/enforcement-local-law-144-automated-employment-decision-tools).

applicable_aedt.name: Automated Employment Decision Tool Subject to LL144

criteria.summary: A computational process (including one derived from AI, machine learning, statistical modeling, or similar techniques) used to substantially assist or replace discretionary decision-making -- covering early-stage screening, final hiring, and promotion decisions -- for a candidate or employee who would work in New York City.
examples: Resume screening or ranking tools that score or rank NYC-based candidates
Automated interview scoring or video-analysis tools used in NYC hiring
Candidate-matching or job-recommendation tools that substantially assist a NYC hiring or promotion decision

citations: NYC Admin. Code § 20-871 (prohibition on use without bias audit, public summary, and notice)

criteria.summary: The tool does not automate, support, substantially assist, or replace discretionary decision-making, and does not materially impact natural persons -- per the DCWP final rule's own carve-out -- or the candidate/employee/position has no New York City nexus.
exemptions: Junk email filters, firewalls, antivirus software, spreadsheets, or other data compilations (the DCWP final rule's own named examples of non-covered tools)
Tools used solely for scheduling or logistics, with no scoring or ranking role in the decision
Positions with no New York City nexus
